edges.description: GALC deficiency leads directly to accumulation of the toxic metabolite psychosine.

GALC deficiency also drives an independent parallel arm of disease: macrophages unable to degrade galactosylceramide are transformed into globoid cells by GalCer exposure, producing secondary neuroinflammation.
Psychosine accumulation is toxic to myelin-producing cells in both CNS and PNS.
Psychosine can drive MMP-3-mediated transformation of microglia into multinucleated globoid cells, connecting the storage metabolite to a hallmark macrophage/microglial lesion.
Psychosine accumulation impairs autophagy and ubiquitin-proteasome handling.
Toxic injury to myelin-producing cells drives downstream demyelination.
CNS demyelination produces the characteristic leukodystrophy phenotype.
Peripheral demyelination causes the peripheral neuropathy phenotype.
Central motor-tract and white-matter demyelination contributes to early spasticity in infantile and later-onset Krabbe disease.
Progressive CNS demyelination and neurodegeneration cause loss of acquired developmental milestones.
Infantile Krabbe disease often first presents with irritability during the rapidly progressive neurodegenerative phase.
Progressive CNS white-matter disease and neurodegeneration are associated with seizures during infantile Krabbe disease.
Visual pathway involvement during CNS demyelination can progress to optic atrophy in a subset of patients.
Progressive neurologic deterioration causes swallowing and feeding impairment, often requiring tube feeding in infantile disease.
Activated glial cells participate in downstream inflammasome-related inflammatory signaling.
Psychosine-driven proteostasis failure contributes to neuroinflammatory glial reactivity; reducing aggregate burden attenuates reactive astrocytes and ameboid microglia in the twitcher model.
Elevated psychosine reports the proximal toxic sphingolipid storage abnormality caused by GALC deficiency.
Low leukocyte or dried-blood-spot GALC enzyme activity directly reports the initiating galactosylceramidase deficiency.

nodes.description: Large deletion encompassing exons 11-17, the most common pathogenic variant in European populations.

GeneReviews lists atypical antipsychotics, multi-drug seizure regimens that cause over-sedation, and routine childhood vaccinations as agents/circumstances to avoid because they can affect cognition, respiratory drive, neurologic decline, or disease progression.

evidence.snippet: Agents/circumstances to avoid: Atypical antipsychotics and multiple medications for seizures can cause over-sedation (affecting cognition, respiratory drive, and rate of neurologic decline). Routine childhood vaccinations can accelerate disease progression.

nodes.description: Krabbe disease causes progressive loss of myelin in the central and peripheral nervous systems.

evidence.snippet: Functional deficiency of GALC is toxic to myelin-producing cells, which leads to progressive demyelination in both the central and peripheral nervous systems.

nodes.description: Loss of previously acquired developmental milestones is a hallmark of infantile Krabbe disease, with progressive loss of motor and cognitive skills following initial normal development.

evidence.snippet: Irritability, abnormalities in movement pattern as well as general developmental regression were the first symptoms in the infantile group

nodes.description: Difficulty with feeding due to bulbar dysfunction and progressive neurological deterioration, often requiring tube feeding in infantile Krabbe disease.

evidence.snippet: Irritability, abnormalities in movement pattern as well as general developmental regression were the first symptoms in the infantile group; disease course was severe with rapid progression, e.g. loss of visual fixation, need for tube feeding and then an early death.
the deletion is approximately 30 kb starting near the middle of the 12 kb intron 10, and includes all of the coding region through exon 17 plus an additional 9 kb

a large deletion, together with a polymorphic C to T transition at position 502 of cDNA (counting from the A of the initiation codon), is responsible for a large number of disease-causing alleles in patients with Krabbe disease
Krabbe Disease (KD) is an autosomal recessive disorder that results from loss-of-function mutations in the GALC gene, which encodes lysosomal enzyme galactosylceramidase (GALC).
GALC | HGNC:4115 | Krabbe disease | MONDO:0009499 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in GALC cause deficiency of the lysosomal enzyme galactosylceramidase.

evidence.snippet: Infantile globoid cell leukodystrophy (GLD, Krabbe disease) is a fatal demyelinating disorder caused by a deficiency in the lysosomal enzyme galactosylceramidase (GALC). GALC deficiency leads to the accumulation of the cytotoxic glycolipid, galactosylsphingosine (psychosine).
homozygous null mutations in GALC lead to deficiency in total GALC protein and activity

readouts.interpretation: Low leukocyte or dried-blood-spot GALC enzyme activity directly reports the initiating galactosylceramidase deficiency.

nodes.description: GALC-deficient macrophages cannot degrade galactosylceramide (GalCer); when exposed to GalCer they are transformed into globoid cells and drive a more severe phenotype, independent of psychosine-induced oligodendrocyte demyelination. This identifies a parallel disease arm driven by macrophage-autonomous GalCer storage and explains why HSCT (delivering healthy phagocytic macrophages) reduces nerve globoid cells without requiring efficient enzyme cross-correction.

evidence.reference_title: Macrophages Expressing GALC Improve Peripheral Krabbe Disease by a Mechanism Independent of Cross-Correction.
evidence.snippet: macrophages require GALC to degrade myelin, as Galc-deficient macrophages are transformed into globoid cells by exposure to galactosylceramide and produce a more severe GLD phenotype

nodes.description: AAV-mediated gene therapy to restore GALC expression is under active investigation. Preclinical studies in mouse and canine models show that CNS-directed AAV-GALC can normalize psychosine and extend survival. Combination of systemic AAV gene therapy with HSCT has shown the most promising results in canine models, with clinical trials underway (NCT04693598, NCT05739643).

evidence.snippet: elevated levels of psychosine were completely normalized in the forebrain region of the treated mice
The median lifespan was extended from 43 days to 78 days (range: 74-88 days) in the AAV1-GALC-treated group

nodes.description: Multinucleated globoid macrophages accumulate in Krabbe disease white matter as a hallmark neuropathologic lesion.

evidence.snippet: an early-infantile case (age of death at 10 months) had about 3-fold increases in both globoid cells, a pathological hallmark for KD

nodes.description: HSCT is the only treatment shown to alter disease course when performed presymptomatically in infantile Krabbe disease or early in later-onset forms. It provides donor-derived enzyme to the CNS via engrafted microglia. For infantile KD, transplantation must occur within the first 30-45 days of life to be effective.

evidence.snippet: Infants who underwent transplantation before the development of symptoms showed progressive central myelination and continued gains in developmental skills, and most had age-appropriate cognitive function and receptive language skills
Children who underwent transplantation after the onset of symptoms had minimal neurologic improvement.

nodes.description: Non-canonical (caspase-11) and canonical (caspase-1) inflammasome pathways and gasdermin D are progressively induced in nervous tissue in Krabbe disease. Caspase-11 localizes to reactive microglia/macrophages and astrocytes, while caspase-1 and gasdermin D are restricted to reactive microglia/macrophages. This inflammasome signature accompanies demyelination and gliosis and may represent a therapeutic target.

evidence.snippet: the expression of pro-inflammatory non-canonical caspase-11, canonical caspase-1, gasdermin D and cognate genes is induced in nervous tissue
Caspase-11 is found in reactive microglia/macrophages as well as astrocytes but caspase-1 and gasdermin D are restricted to reactive microglia/macrophages

nodes.description: Extreme irritability is often the earliest clinical feature in infantile Krabbe disease, frequently preceding other neurological signs.

evidence.snippet: Irritability, abnormalities in movement pattern as well as general developmental regression were the first symptoms in the infantile group

nodes.description: Progressive white matter degeneration visible on MRI as symmetric signal abnormalities in cerebral white matter, with eventual atrophy.

evidence.snippet: The different forms of the disease were characterized by different MRI patterns (infantile: diffuse white matter involvement and cerebellar structures specifically affected, later onset: parieto-occipital white matter and splenium affected, adult: motor tracts specifically affected).

nodes.description: Krabbe disease triggers marked activation of microglia and astrocytes in affected white matter.

evidence.snippet: The astrocytes and microglia reactivity were attenuated in that reactive astrocytes, ameboid microglia, and globoid cells were reduced in the brains of rapamycin-treated twitcher mice

nodes.description: Psychosine toxicity injures myelin-producing oligodendrocytes and Schwann cells.

evidence.snippet: The concentration of psychosine will steadily increase and reach toxic levels and kill the oligodendroglial cells. This results in an arrest of the galactosylceramide biosynthesis.

nodes.description: Progressive optic nerve demyelination leads to optic atrophy and visual impairment, particularly in infantile Krabbe disease.

evidence.snippet: Optic atrophy was uncommon in our series (present only in a single case).

nodes.description: Demyelinating peripheral neuropathy due to Schwann cell loss, manifesting as hyporeflexia and reduced nerve conduction velocities.

evidence.snippet: Functional deficiency of GALC is toxic to myelin-producing cells, which leads to progressive demyelination in both the central and peripheral nervous systems.

nodes.description: Psychosine causes dysfunction of autophagy and the ubiquitin-proteasome system, leading to accumulation of insoluble ubiquitinated protein aggregates in the brain. This proteostasis failure contributes to neurodegeneration and can be partially rescued by mTOR inhibition with rapamycin.

evidence.snippet: psychosine causes dysfunction of autophagy and the ubiquitin-proteasome system underlying the pathogenesis of globoid cell leukodystrophy (GLD), a devastating lysosomal storage disease complicated by global demyelination
significantly reduced the accumulation of insoluble ubiquitinated protein and the formation of ubiquitin aggregates
GALC deficiency leads to the accumulation of the cytotoxic glycolipid, galactosylsphingosine (psychosine)
a two-tiered strategy based on psychosine (PSY) as the determinant if an NBS result is positive or negative after a first-tier test revealed decreased galactocerebrosidase activity

readouts.interpretation: Elevated psychosine reports the proximal toxic sphingolipid storage abnormality caused by GALC deficiency.

nodes.description: Psychosine is the key toxic lipid that accumulates in Krabbe disease.

evidence.snippet: Infantile globoid cell leukodystrophy (GLD, Krabbe disease) is a fatal demyelinating disorder caused by a deficiency in the lysosomal enzyme galactosylceramidase (GALC). GALC deficiency leads to the accumulation of the cytotoxic glycolipid, galactosylsphingosine (psychosine).
psychosine is generated catabolically through the deacylation of galactosylceramide by acid ceramidase (ACDase)

nodes.description: Seizures occur in Krabbe disease, particularly in advanced stages. Uncontrolled seizures are a frequent terminal event in infantile Krabbe disease.

evidence.snippet: Most of the children with infantile disease presented with neurodegeneration, seizures or fever

nodes.description: Progressive increase in muscle tone due to upper motor neuron involvement from white matter destruction.

evidence.snippet: Developmental and disease trajectories were described based on the acquisition and loss of milestones as well as the time of first clearly identifiable symptoms and needs such as spasticity, seizures and tube feeding.

nodes.description: Symptomatic management including physical therapy, antispasmodics, nutritional support, and seizure management.

evidence.snippet: Treatment of a child who is symptomatic before age six months is supportive and focused on increasing the quality of life and avoiding complications.
